Summary: TCF7L2 has a genetic role in type 2 diabetes across multiple populations and its association with T2D has been consistently replicated. A meta-analysis reported that TCF7L2 variants act under a multiplicative genetic model and are involved in nearly one-fifth of T2D cases. TCF7L2 risk variants were associated with impaired glucose regulation, reduced insulin secretion, and a more rapid decline in pancreatic β‑cell function. Variant rs290487 contributed to insulin resistance via Wnt signaling. In human cohort studies TCF7L2 risk variants predicted T2D onset and indicated familial genetic predisposition to developing impaired glycemia over time. The relevance of ethnic differences remained to be determined and resequencing in diverse ethnic groups was proposed to identify common and rare variants. The role of TCF7L2 variants in complex regulatory functions has been little explored and further functional and physiological studies were required. Developing therapies that modulate TCF7L2 expression was proposed as a potential means to treat T2D patients carrying a TCF7L2 risk allele.
References: Del Bosque-Plata, L., Martínez-Martínez, E., Espinoza-Camacho, M. Á., & Gragnoli, C. (2021). The Role of TCF7L2 in Type 2 Diabetes. Diabetes, 70(6), 1220-1228. https://doi.org/10.2337/db20-0573
